title: Complementary-polarity double-layer LiTaO3 resonators for symmetry-selective SH2 excitation with ultrahigh electromechanical coupling (kt^2 = 25.7%)
authors: Hao Yan, Zhen-hui Qin, Zhi-Wen Wang, Shu-Mao Wu, Chen-Bei Hao, Hua-Yang Chen, Sheng-Nan Liang, Ke Chen, Si-Yuan Yu, Yan-Feng Chen
abstract: We report a structurally simple double-layer lithium tantalate (LiTaO3) bulk acoustic resonator that enables symmetry-selective excitation of the second-order thickness-shear (SH2) mode with ultrahigh electromechanical coupling. Two 31 deg Y-oriented single-crystal LiTaO3 films are rotation-bonded with complementary polarization (+X/-X) and driven by a longitudinal electric field. Matching between the effective piezoelectric symmetry and the SH2 mode yields an effective electromechanical coupling coefficient of kt^2 = 25.7% at 5.24 MHz. To our knowledge, this is the highest kt^2 reported for a LiTaO3 resonator architecture to date. The measured response is dominated by the target SH2 mode, with only weak parasitic features in the operating band. The structure is also tunable: the resonance frequency and coupling can be adjusted through geometric parameters while maintaining stable modal behavior, indicating good process tolerance. Finite-element analysis further suggests straightforward frequency scaling beyond 5 GHz by reducing the film and electrode thickness while preserving approximately 25% kt^2. In addition, introducing a SiO2 compensation layer is predicted to improve the temperature coefficient of frequency to approximately -25 ppm/deg C. These results establish complementary-polarity double-layer LiTaO3 as a practical platform for high-coupling, spurious-suppressed acoustic resonators and provide a scalable route toward wideband ultrasonic resonators, filters, and related transducers.
summary: This paper presents a new way to build lithium tantalate (LiTaO3) acoustic resonators by bonding two layers with opposite polarities. This specific arrangement allows for the selective excitation of a desired mode (SH2) while canceling out unwanted interference modes. The result is a record-high electromechanical coupling of 25.7%, which is crucial for creating wideband ultrasonic filters and sensors. The design is also scalable to much higher frequencies and can be made temperature-stable using a compensation layer.
main_problem: The need to increase the electromechanical coupling coefficient (kt^2) in LiTaO3 resonators for wider bandwidth applications while simultaneously suppressing unwanted spurious modes and maintaining temperature stability.
main_result: The development of a double-layer LiTaO3 resonator with complementary polarization that achieves an ultrahigh electromechanical coupling of 25.7% and suppresses parasitic modes through symmetry-selective excitation.
method: The researchers used rotation-bonding of two 31 deg Y-oriented LiTaO3 films with complementary (+X/-X) polarization, followed by finite-element analysis (FEA) to predict frequency scaling and temperature compensation.
model_or_system: A double-layer bulk acoustic resonator consisting of two 365 um thick LiTaO3 plates rotation-bonded with an in-plane relative rotation of 121 degrees, featuring aluminum electrodes for longitudinal electric field driving.
key_observables: Electromechanical coupling coefficient (kt^2), resonance frequency (fs), quality factors (Qs and Qp), and temperature coefficient of frequency (TCF).
important_parameters: kt^2 = 25.7%, resonance frequency = 5.24 MHz, rotation angle = 121 degrees, and predicted TCF of -25 ppm/deg C with SiO2 compensation.
main_assumptions: The frequency scaling assumes the ability to reduce film and electrode thickness while preserving the coupling coefficient; temperature modeling assumes known temperature-dependent material constants.
figures_summary: Fig 1 shows the 3D and cross-sectional diagrams of the resonator; Fig 2 displays piezoelectric coefficients at different rotation angles, admittance spectra for single and double-layer resonators, and displacement-field distributions for primary and spurious modes.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of coupling and spurious modes, describes the fabrication and structural design of the bilayer resonator, presents experimental measurements of coupling and frequency, provides FEA simulations for scaling and temperature stability, and concludes with potential applications.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
